frameworks.name: drift-diffusion / bounded evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Noisy samples of evidence accumulate to a bound during perceptual decision formation.

frameworks.name: discrete stepping model of LIP firing rates
frameworks.one_line: Single-trial rates jump from intermediate to low/high level at a random time.

frameworks.name: Bayesian model comparison
frameworks.one_line: Used by the challenged study to rank stepping above ramping.

core_question: Does single-trial LIP spiking really favor a discrete stepping account over drift-diffusion?
key_assumptions: LIP firing reflects the decision variable during deliberation
Trial-averaged ramps could arise from either diffusion or averaged steps
Model comparison is valid only if each model is faithfully specified
manipulations: Re-specification of the drift-diffusion model used in the prior comparison
Re-analysis of LIP single-trial spike sequences under corrected diffusion
key_findings: A shortcoming in the mathematical depiction of drift-diffusion biased the original model comparison
Once corrected, the evidence no longer favors stepping over diffusion-like accumulation
interpretations: There is currently no compelling evidence that single-trial LIP activity steps rather than ramps
